figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic overview of the GE-BiCross architecture with a hierarchical 312 mixture-of-experts (MoE) framework for genomic–environmental interaction 313 modeling. 314 The GE-BiCross model comprises three major components: (i) a genomic tokenization 315 layer that transforms single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) markers into position-316 aware embeddings, together with an environmental encoder for multi-modal 317 environmental inputs, including meteorological, soil, and management variables; (ii) a 318

Figure 2. Performance comparison of genomic prediction models across six 327 maize traits. 328 The figure compares the predictive performance of six models, including GE-BiCross, 329 Random Forest (RF), Gradient Boosting Trees (GBT), Genomic Best Linear Unbiased 330 Prediction (GBLUP), GEFormer, and K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), across six maize 331 traits: grain yield, days to silking, days to pollen shed, plant height, grain moisture, and 332 ear height. Predictive performance is evaluated using the coefficient of determination 333 (R²). 334

Figure 3. Ablation analysis of the GE-BiCross model across six maize traits. 341 The heatmap shows the predictive performance of the full GE-BiCross model and three 342 ablated variants: GE-BiCross without the mixture-of-experts (MoE) module, GE-343 BiCross without the effect separation module, and GE-BiCross without token fusion. 344 Model performance is compared across six maize traits, including grain yield, days to 345 silking, days to pollen shed, plant height, grain moisture, and ear height, to illustrate 346 the contribution of individual architectural components. 347
